sentences.text: Yes.
We were in the ghetto until they finished building the railroad.
And while we were there, of course everybody was working really hard.
There were-- there were tortures.
There were-- there were a lot of-- a lot of people died.
A lot of people starved.
A lot of people had no medication.
There was very little that we got.
Packages were coming in from the outside.
And they said that was our ration, but I-- I-- we-- we thought that the Jews that were still in the city were-- were doing that, they were sending in.
For one, I know my father had a cousin that lived all his life in Munkacs, and he owned a store.
It was called the [NON-ENGLISH].
That means the yellow store.
And they sold materials like for clothing and I don't know what you call that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You know for clothing, and for different things-- all kind of materials.
And we knew that they were still home, they weren't taken to the ghetto yet.
And my father's cousin that had the resort in Polana, he also lived in Munkacs in the hotel, and they were still home.
And so they were sending us food from the outside into the ghetto.
Then when the railroad got finished, when they finished the railroad coming into the ghetto, nobody was allowed to come in, or we weren't getting any communications from the outside.
In fact, they were bringing people in from the city into the ghetto and boarding the-- the railroad.
And again, we were told that we-- now we're going to Tokaj and we were going to make wine in Tokaj until the war is over.
And the reason we're leaving here is because the conditions are not-- not-- not good enough for waiting out the war here.
Of course, everybody never questioned that and--
About a week, maybe a little longer.
It's not-- not much more than that.
Well, we-- we helped, you know, keep things clean.
I was going through the water to wash things like towels and sheets and our clothes, and just hiding from places, hiding so we weren't taken away, or putting into some kind of a-- a torture.
So we stayed close with the family.
And in-- in where we were, we were not noticeable.
But one day I was by the water, and I was washing at the water, and the guards-- there were guards everywhere.
And this one guard came over, and he said to me, what are you doing here?
Why are you here?
And I says, because my family is here.
And he said, your family is here?
Are you sure that you didn't come here because you had a very close Jewish friend, because you are not Jewish?
I says, but I am Jewish and my family is Jewish, and I am here with my family.
And he said, I don't believe you.
But if you want to come out here, I will take you back home to your family.
And I said no, my family is here.
And I came home and I told my mother.
And I was not quite 13 years old yet, and I didn't understand why he said that to me.
So I came home-- I came back into-- into our place, and I said to my mother what he said.
And he said-- she said to me, you will-- you're not allowed to go there anymore.
She was afraid that maybe he was trying to-- I don't know why.
But I was not allowed to go to the water to wash anything there anymore.
And so we were pretty much-- did nothing and did everything.
Did you witness, personally, any atrocities, any--
In the ghetto?
Only hard labor.
Only I saw my father and my brother come home, and how they were-- they had wounds and they were hurt from the stones or whatever they were working at.
And they were sick and tired, and all that.
I didn't witness anything until the trains came, and then I saw a lot.

Then when the trains came into the ghetto, and they-- they were-- they were taking people away, and everybody was running around everywhere just to gather the families together to be-- nobody had any objection of going, but they just wanted to go together with their families.
But that wasn't important to the guards.
They just-- whoever they found, they just pushed them on the trains to fill up the train.
The doors closed.
Whether the families were together or one person from the family was on a train, that didn't matter.
And then the train just went.
And-- and then another train came, and another came until they emptied the camp.
And then we were on our way to Tokaj to make wine, is what they said.
Yeah.
They told us to take all our valuables again, all our valuable possessions.
But again, my grandmother and my mother, they-- they were sorting out things while we were in the ghetto to put together for us and to take with us, and-- and clothing.
Mainly they were trying to put a lot of clothing on our bodies so we don't have heavy things to carry, because they put other things in our-- in our suitcases to carry.
So they put-- like I had like five or six skirts on me and tops, and my head was covered, like they put these babushkas on my head.
And we-- we were just dressed, overly dressed, so we could have extra stuff too, because we were only allowed to take so much.
And the suitcases were for other things.
But one thing my mother did is she-- she took diamonds, and they-- and she rolled diamonds into one of my skirts and my sister.
Of course, my sister was only 4 1/2 years old.
But I guess that was a way of putting-- preserving things, or-- you know.

And she said to me to take care of that one skirt in case I have to go to work in a different place where she's going to work, then to take care of that skirt, and during the time if I don't have anything to eat, to take out the diamonds and buy bread.
That's what that is for, but be very careful not to lose it.
So I knew that there was that in the-- in the bottom of my skirt.
And-- and we went on a train.
And we were fortunate that we were-- we were trying to stay back, not to get on a train before our cousins came in from the city and we were going to go together with them.
But we heard-- we didn't see it, but we heard that a man came in from the city and he knew my father.
And he said, don't wait for your cousin.
He's not going to be here.
And he said, why?
They tortured him and his family, and they shot him in the middle of the square.
They tortured his two daughters to death and he watched.
And then they shot the rest of the family in the square of the city, and then-- because he was one of the people that picked up the bodies and buried them at the cemetery before they took him away.
And he came into the ghetto, and when he saw my father, and he knew my father was waiting for them to come into the ghetto so we can go together, he says-- he told him what happened to them.
And I don't know what happened to my other-- to my father's other cousin, whether they-- they came into the ghetto and went to the concentration camp.
But I never saw them again either.
This was in Munkacs.
And-- and so we went on the train and we started on our journey-- thinking that we're going to Tokaj.

And after a couple of days, my father-- we were in closed wagons, but the only thing-- the outside-- we had a crack in the-- in the wall on the wagon, so somebody was able to-- at the time to look outside and-- and maybe see a glimpse of something that they can recognize.
And a couple of days later, my father said we are not going to Tokaj.
We just left our borders.
We are out of the country and we are heading towards Poland.
See, he recognized the route.
And so everybody started to remember.
Now all the adults were like very, very upset in the train.
And of course, the train was so packed we had no room to move around.
I was sitting against the wall, on my suitcase.
My sister was sitting on one-- on one leg.
My brother was sitting on my other leg.
And we were changing places.
My mother and father were standing.
My grandmother and my uncle, they were standing around us.
And we were changing like places to sit on the suitcase and holding the children, because the children were little.
They didn't-- and they were-- they were hungry and they were tired and everybody was crying.
And it was just terrible.
And especially when everybody knew that we left the country, everybody was very panicky.
Everybody was trying to reorganize.
They didn't know what, but they started to remember what they heard, and what they-- what they heard from the horse's mouth, the people that came over from Poland during the 1939, '40, and '41.
And they-- they were even afraid to even think about it, that you can could see the expressions on their face.
Everybody was very scared.
And from then on in, it was-- it was terrible, because people were dying from exhaustion, from hunger.
The trains never opened.
The doors never opened to let us out for-- to go to the bathroom.
There was one pail.
I think it was a pail, or something that looked like a pail.
And when that filled up, that was it.
Everybody went to the bathroom where they sat, or they stood, or they died.
And the older people couldn't make it.
They never made it into, you know, the-- the train.
After a couple of days, they were gone.
So we were in the train with sick people, with dead people, with old people, with children.
Children were crying constantly, constantly crying to go home, to go home, when we going to go home?
[CRYING]
I was holding my sister.
She-- [CRYING] she was begging me for water, and I couldn't give her any water.
[CRYING] And the parents were trying to console us and promise that it'll be better, it'll be better, tomorrow will be better, but tomorrow was so long away.
It was like it never was going to come.

And so after a while, we were in that train for so long and it was so horrible that we didn't care where we went, as long as they'd just open the door so we could just breathe some air or see some light.
And-- and the-- the train did stop.
They did open the door.
[CRYING] And then we didn't see much light.
[CRYING] When they opened the doors, they didn't let us off right away.
They said only the-- the women and the children should come off the train, and the boys and the men should stay behind.
So of course, being gentlemen, they let the women and the children go first.
And when we got off the-- the wagon, they just marched us.
My mother was holding my-- my little brother in her arms.
In the other arm, she held my sister's hand.
And I was holding my sister's hand, so we walked together.
My two brothers were-- stayed back with my father.
So we were walking and then we-- and-- and these people in black uniforms [CRYING] with rifles yelling, [NON-ENGLISH].
And we moved into this one direction, and then there was this one man with a baton, like a magic wand in his hand.
And he was flipping that thing back and forth, telling people where to go.

When we got closer to him, he told my mother to put the children down on the floor, and she wouldn't-- she wouldn't do it, so she hung on to-- so he took this little thing and he split me away from my sister.
And he pushed me over to the side, and my mother and the children to the other side.
And I looked back, and I said I want to go with my mother, and I screamed.
And then the guard took me, and he was pushing me in this direction.
And my mother was hollering at me, don't worry.
I'll see you tomorrow.
Just don't-- just don't cry.
Everything'll be OK.
I'll see you tomorrow.
[CRYING] And then she-- she just went away.
[CRYING] And-- and I was taken to these-- a whole bunch of people that I didn't know.
We were marched.
We were walking, another way.
On the way, I saw wires on each side of us.
And there were people behind the wires with shaved heads and one piece of clothing on their body.
And-- and I said, what is this?
Where are we going?
And everybody said, it's an insane asylum.
This is not for us.
We're going somewhere.
These people are insane.
This is a hospital.
And we kept going.
And then we saw these black trucks.
They were lined up on a road just-- the train was on this side that we came off, and these black trucks were lined up on this side.
And as we were going, see, my mother and the children went that away, and I went that away.
Then-- then as we were coming down almost to the end of the road, I looked on the side of the road, and there was my mother and the children.
They were going on the truck.
And I said, oh good, they don't have to walk.
It'll be so good for them to go on the-- you know, they're taking-- oh, how nice, you know.
I thought it was so great that they were getting on a truck.
[CRYING] And then we never saw them again.
And then we found out where those trucks were going.
But I was-- I was a little bit at peace, knowing that they were a little bit better taken care of.
And then us-- they took us maybe-- well, we got to this big hall.
It looked like such a humongous place, but we were pushed together, like in like half of the room.
But then I realized that there was very few of us there, compared to what came on the train.
I mean, almost a half a ghetto was on the train, and there was just one room full of us.
And-- but I didn't have time to speculate or look around for anything, because they ordered us to take off our clothes.
There was a row of gestapo on one side of the room, and then there was a-- a row of benches that was separating us from them.
And they were standing with big German shepherd, about this big.
I have never seen such a big dog in my whole life.
They were holding them real close by there-- you know, on a-- on a collar.
And they were telling us to take our clothes off, so we all took our clothes off, and-- up to the underwear.
And they said to take everybody-- everything off, and then people weren't taking their everything off, because they weren't going to be exposed like that to-- you know, in front of one another.
So they let the dogs at them.
And at the first-- you know, the first group of people got all chewed up by the dogs, and then everybody took their clothes off after that, of course.
Then they ordered us to put our clothes in-- pick up our clothes in one arm and our shoes in the other arm, and then just turn to the right.
And we were being led to another section.
And then I remembered my mother had put stuff in my skirt, so I took the skirt and I opened it up and I took the diamonds out.
And I held them in my hand, in my fist.
And I was walking with my clothes in one hand, and the shoes in the other hand.
And then we came to a different section, and they told us to drop the clothes in the bin-- in one bin, and the shoes in the other bin.
And I did that.
And I still held on to the diamonds.
And then I saw in front of me they took the people in front of me and they were examining them.
And they were putting their hands up their-- their privates and looking for things.
And I was holding these diamonds, and they were announcing, anybody have any possessions, to drop it in this bin.
And I had these diamonds and I remember my mother saying this is to buy bread when you're hungry.
And I didn't know how to save them, so I put them in my mouth.
[CRYING] And then when they-- they shaved my head.
And then the next section, they were looking for gold teeth, so they-- we didn't know what they were looking for, but they were telling people to open their mouth.
And I had the diamonds in my mouth, so I didn't know what to do.
I was like watched by that time.
And it was the last warning, was to put their possessions, because if not, we're going to be punished.
And I had these diamonds in my mouth, so I swallowed them.
I was afraid to be caught with them, so I swallowed them.
And then we went into another room.
They told us that we were going to get a shower.
And we did get a shower, a little trickle of a shower.
And then there was a pile of clothing.
They shaved our heads.
There was a pile of clothing by that opening, and they ordered everybody to take one piece of clothing.
And so whatever you got, you got.
And I got a man's pajama top.
So being I was so little, it covered me up to here.
And so my body was covered.
A lot of people only got a bottom.
A lot of people-- it was-- it was-- I looked at the people, and I didn't recognize anybody anymore.
And my cousin's sister-- well, actually, she was my father's cousin, and her sister were in-- in the group with me.
And I didn't know.
I didn't recognize them.
I didn't know which ones they were, because they shaved their heads.
They took their clothing away.
And when we picked our clothes, they led us through this door, and there was a mirror by the door so we can see each other, so we can see what we look like.
Like I don't know why, but that was the first step of dehumanization that we realized that they don't care about us.
And we went through that door into like a courtyard.
And from that courtyard, I saw the gas chamber.
But I didn't know at that time what it was.
But I saw my mother and the children again, coming off the truck and going behind this little building.
And later on I found out that was the gas chamber.
And they told us to put our clothes on and they gave us a pair of wooden shoes.
And they lined us up in-- in a row of five, and they started to lead us out of that area, again back on the road that we were when we came off the train, only on a different part of it, I think.
But it looked the same, the same road.
And we went into-- they led us into a-- to a camp with people that we thought it was they were insane.
And we looked at those people, and I says, oh my God, they think we are insane, and we're in this hospital with all these insane people.
And then I remembered I saw myself in the mirror, I looked like that too.
And I said, oh my god.
What is this?
Where are we?
Why are we here?
And then the-- the panic started.
But it was too late.
We were behind barbed wires.
The electricity went on.
All of a sudden, there were lights on the wires.
And the people that were inside-- we were put in a barracks.
We were put in-- you know, we walked into this barrack.
And these-- the capos, they were our leaders.
They were in charge of the barrack, and they were so mean.
Where?
Oh, we arrived to Birkenau.
And in Birkenau is where we got separated by the train, by the railroad train, and-- and we marched down one road and my parents and my-- I never saw my father and the-- the boys after that.
But somebody said they went on a truck, you know, they'll be OK, before we realized where that truck went.
So that made me feel a little bit better before we really found out what really happened to them.
But we didn't find out for a couple of days.
We-- when we came into the camp and they brought me in with the group to [NON-ENGLISH].
That's C in-- in Birkenau.
And I was-- they put us into this barrack, and I think it was barrack number 24.
I'm not sure.
I know I was at one time or another in a barrack 24, because I was in several during my stay there.
So I think the first one could have been 24.
But that wasn't even important, because it wasn't much we could have done about it to change it.
But when they opened the doors to the barrack, they told us that 10 each, 10 is to go into the bunk.

There were three-story wooden bunks in the barrack with a brick thing going in the middle from one end of the barrack to the other, looking like a large brick bench, which was supposed to be a stove to keep the barrack warm.
Of course, they never heated the barrack.
They never put the stove on, but it was there.
They told us that 10 people are to share one bunk.
There was one blanket in some of the bunks, not every bunk, so we didn't have a blanket.
I was on the third bunk, on the-- on the third floor, actually.
And there were 10 of us in the bunk.
And there were 10 of us in the middle and 10 of us on the bottom.
But the youngest ones had to go up, because we were able to climb up every time we had to go up and down, so we were up on the third-- on the third bunk.
And I was there with my father's cousin and her sister.
And they started to take us out to roll call, to appel, between the two barracks immediately after everybody went in.
And they assigned-- they-- everybody knew which bunk was his.
And then they took us out and immediately we were standing in mud to our knees outside, and they were taking count.
And my cousin's-- my father's cousin, she was like my mother's age.
She was about 32 years old.
She said, if they don't let me out by morning, I am going to kill myself.
I am not going to be here.
And her sister said, there is no way you're going to kill yourself, and they're not going to let you out yet.
They will eventually, but not yet.
And she-- from that moment on, she-- she just couldn't take that.
She didn't-- she did not want to live.
She did not want to live where she was.
And of course, there was very little food.
We got one slice of bread.
It was maybe a quarter of an inch thick.
It was so horrible.
At that time, we were still able to distinguish how bad or good it was, because we weren't-- we-- we weren't that hungry yet.
And we had-- they gave us a bowl, a little bowl.
If you lost your bowl, you didn't have any food.
So if people didn't have a bowl, because some people didn't get to get a bowl, what they did is that they gave us a bucket of soup that was for the 10 people that were in the-- in the bunk.
And we each had to share that, so everybody took a sip and passed it to the other one.
And we sipped and we passed it around.
And God forbid somebody took a bigger sip.
So, my cousin, she didn't want to live.
So her sister-- her name was Faigy and my father's cousin's name was [NON-ENGLISH].
We used to call her, you know, [NON-ENGLISH], 0 like an aunt, you know.
And she-- we used to give up our sip and give it to her so she can live.
So we-- we were-- we were hungry most of the time, trying to keep her alive.
But she died.
She died in a very short time.
